past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well controlled on medication
Mild cervical spondylosis with intermittent neck stiffness, managed conservatively
Right clavicle fracture at age 32 from cycling accident, healed without surgical intervention
family_history: Father died of colon cancer at age 74
Sister with breast cancer diagnosed at 55
No family history of brain tumors or neurological diseases

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, 2-3 times per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story apartment
social_history.exercise: Avid amateur cyclist, rides 80-100 km per week; significantly reduced activity over the past month due to symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mildly slow in responses. Language fluent with occasional word-finding pauses. Able to follow three-step commands. Recall 2/3 objects at 5 minutes. Attention mildly impaired on serial sevens with two errors. Judgment and insight generally preserved.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation except for subtle left inferior quadrantanopia. Fundoscopic exam reveals early bilateral papilledema. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Mild left central facial droop. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 deltoid, 4/5 biceps, 4+/5 triceps, 4/5 grip, 4/5 finger abduction. Left lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5-/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Pronator drift present on the left. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Decreased graphesthesia in left hand. Proprioception intact at the great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at the ankles bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 3+ with extensor plantar response (Babinski sign) on the left. Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric on the left, intact on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently but with left arm swing reduction and mild left foot drag. Slight circumduction of the left lower extremity. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. Early papilledema present bilaterally. During the ED evaluation, one witnessed episode of left hand tonic posturing lasting approximately 45 seconds with secondary generalization into a bilateral tonic-clonic seizure lasting 75 seconds. The patient was postictal for approximately 10 minutes.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive right-sided headaches, left arm weakness, and a first-time seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Paolo Feretti is a 59-year-old right-handed man and avid amateur cyclist who presents to the emergency department following a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. His wife reports that approximately four weeks ago he began experiencing intermittent tingling and burning pain in his left hand and forearm. Initially, he and his general practitioner attributed these symptoms to nerve compression related to prolonged cycling posture on his road bike, and he was referred for physiotherapy. Over the following two weeks, the paresthesias became more frequent and intense, occurring not only during cycling but also at rest, and were accompanied by brief involuntary twitching movements of his left hand lasting seconds at a time. Three weeks ago, he developed a progressive right-sided headache that was worst in the early morning hours and occasionally woke him from sleep. Over-the-counter ibuprofen provided only partial relief. Approximately two weeks ago, his wife began noticing that he had difficulty using his left hand for fine motor tasks such as buttoning shirts and typing on his keyboard. He also appeared more forgetful and slower in his thinking over the past ten days, occasionally losing track of conversations and misplacing items. This morning at approximately 08:30, while sitting at the breakfast table, his wife witnessed his left hand begin to assume a tonic posture, followed within seconds by rhythmic clonic jerking that spread to involve the entire left arm and then progressed to a generalized convulsion lasting approximately 75 seconds. He was confused and somnolent for about ten minutes afterward. EMS was called and he was transported to the emergency department. On arrival, he was alert and conversant but had a persistent right-sided headache and mild left arm weakness. He denies any prior seizure history, recent head trauma, fever, vision changes, nausea, or vomiting. He reports no recent weight loss or night sweats. A non-contrast CT scan obtained in the emergency department demonstrated a large heterogeneously hyperdense mass in the right frontal lobe with significant surrounding hypodensity suggestive of vasogenic edema and approximately 9 mm of leftward midline shift. He was started on intravenous dexamethasone and levetiracetam, and admitted to the neurology service for further evaluation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8.5 Hz alpha rhythm, better organized over the left hemisphere
background.symmetry: Marked asymmetry with amplitude attenuation and continuous slowing over the right frontoparietal region
background.reactivity: Posterior dominant rhythm attenuates with eye opening bilaterally

findings.location: Right frontal and frontoparietal region (F4, C4, Fp2, F8)
findings.morphology: High-amplitude polymorphic slow waves with superimposed fast activity
Phase-reversing sharp waves with aftergoing slow wave, maximal at F4
findings.state: Both wakefulness and drowsiness, more prominent in drowsiness

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, does not significantly obscure interpretation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating continuous right frontal and frontoparietal polymorphic delta activity with frequent right frontocentral sharp waves. There is marked background asymmetry with attenuation over the right hemisphere. The sharp wave localization correlates with the clinical seizure semiology of left-sided motor onset. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to clinical suspicion of elevated intracranial pressure.

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontal lobe, centered in the superior and middle frontal gyri with extension into the precentral gyrus and subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous signal, predominantly hypointense with foci of intrinsic T1 hyperintensity along the posterior margin suggesting hemorrhagic or proteinaceous components
signal_characteristics.T2: Mixed signal intensity; central T2 hyperintensity consistent with necrosis or cystic change surrounded by heterogeneous intermediate signal
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive perilesional FLAIR hyperintensity extending through the right frontal white matter, corona radiata, and into the anterior limb of the internal capsule
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion at the thick enhancing rim consistent with high cellularity; central necrotic core shows facilitated diffusion
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with multiple nodular enhancing components, most prominent along the medial and inferior margins; no enhancement within the necrotic core

findings.mass_effect: Effacement of the right frontal horn of the lateral ventricle. Approximately 9 mm leftward midline shift at the level of the septum pellucidum. Compression of the right caudate head. Sulcal effacement over the right frontal and parietal convexity.
findings.borders: Irregular and infiltrative margins with indistinct interface with surrounding brain parenchyma, suggesting parenchymal invasion

findings.location: Right frontal white matter, extending into the corona radiata, anterior limb of internal capsule, and genu of the corpus callosum

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion within the edematous regions

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to overall mass effect and midline shift
findings.borders: Finger-like projections along white matter tracts characteristic of vasogenic edema

volumetrics.lesion_volume: Approximately 66 cm3 including enhancing and necrotic components
volumetrics.edema_volume: Approximately 95 cm3 of perilesional FLAIR signal abnormality

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the brain parenchyma
Mild chronic microvascular ischemic changes in the periventricular white matter bilaterally
No hydrocephalus despite significant midline shift
Major dural venous sinuses are patent on post-contrast images
Mild mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus (incidental)
mri.impression: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontal mass (5.6 x 5.1 x 4.4 cm) with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, extensive surrounding vasogenic edema, and 9 mm leftward midline shift. The lesion extends into the precentral gyrus, which has implications for surgical planning. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Mild left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
ecg.interpretation: No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia detected.

findings.region: Enhancing rim of the right frontal mass
findings.signal: rCBV_ratio: 4.1 (reference: normal brain = 1.0; high-grade glioma threshold > 1.75); rCBF: Elevated in enhancing components relative to contralateral normal white matter; Ktrans: Elevated, indicating significant blood-brain barrier disruption in the enhancing rim

findings.region: Central necrotic core of the mass
findings.signal: rCBV_ratio: 0.3 (markedly reduced, consistent with necrosis and absence of viable tumor vasculature)

output.impression: The central necrotic core shows markedly reduced perfusion. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Right frontal mass, enhancing rim voxel
findings.signal: choline: Markedly elevated (Cho/Cr ratio 3.8, normal < 1.5); NAA: Markedly reduced (NAA/Cr ratio 0.4, normal > 1.5), indicating neuronal loss or replacement by tumor; lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peak indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism; myoinositol: Mildly elevated
findings.description: Abnormal metabolite ratios within the enhancing mass

output.impression: See findings above. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT_staging_results.value: No pulmonary masses or nodules. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal. No pleural effusion.
Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenals, and kidneys unremarkable. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No suspicious masses.
No pelvic masses. Prostate mildly enlarged, age-appropriate. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Bladder normal.

Neuropathology.value: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and large areas of pseudopalisading necrosis

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Histology Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (>10 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and large areas of pseudopalisading necrosis narrative (H), MGMT promoter methylation Methylated (42% methylation by pyrosequencing) molecular (H), TERT promoter mutation Present (C228T) molecular (H), EGFR amplification Present FISH (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 35% % (H).

findings.signal: activation: Robust BOLD activation in left primary motor cortex during right hand motor task; laterality_index: Strongly left lateralized for right hand motor function

findings.region: Right precentral gyrus, posterior margin of the tumor
findings.signal: activation: Reduced but present BOLD activation along the posterolateral margin of the mass during left hand motor task; activation foci are displaced posteriorly and laterally by mass effect; distance_to_tumor: Approximately 8 mm from the enhancing tumor margin to the nearest motor activation cluster

findings.region: Left inferior frontal gyrus (Broca's area) and left superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke's area)
findings.signal: activation: Robust BOLD activation during language tasks; laterality_index: 0.82 (strongly left lateralized for language)

output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates displacement of right primary motor cortex posterolaterally by the mass with preserved but reduced activation approximately 8 mm from the enhancing margin. Language is strongly left lateralized. The corticospinal tract is displaced but appears intact on DTI tractography. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: glioblastoma standard treatment protocol Stupp temozolomide

results.title: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.summary: Landmark trial establishing the Stupp protocol: maximal safe resection followed by radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with concurrent daily temozolomide (75 mg/m2) followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2, 5/28 days). Improved median OS from 12.1 to 14.6 months. MGMT methylation predicted benefit.

results.title: Effects of radiotherapy with concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide versus radiotherapy alone on survival in glioblastoma: 5-year follow-up
results.authors: Stupp R, Hegi ME, Mason WP, et al.
results.summary: Long-term follow-up confirmed durable survival benefit with combined chemoradiation. 5-year OS rate 9.8% vs 1.9% with radiotherapy alone. MGMT promoter methylation associated with strongest benefit (median OS 21.7 vs 15.3 months for unmethylated).

results.title: Tumor treating fields plus maintenance temozolomide for glioblastoma: the EF-14 trial
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.summary: Addition of tumor treating fields (TTFields) to maintenance temozolomide improved median PFS (6.7 vs 4.0 months) and OS (20.9 vs 16.0 months) compared to temozolomide alone.

output.summary: Standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma is the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy with daily temozolomide 75 mg/m2) and 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2). MGMT promoter methylation is the strongest predictor of temozolomide benefit. Tumor treating fields (TTFields) can be added during maintenance for further survival benefit. Median overall survival with standard treatment is approximately 14-16 months.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily + Dexamethasone 4 mg every 6 hours
interactions: No clinically significant pharmacokinetic interactions between levetiracetam and dexamethasone
Dexamethasone does not significantly induce levetiracetam metabolism (levetiracetam undergoes minimal hepatic metabolism)
warnings: Dexamethasone may cause hyperglycemia; monitor blood glucose especially given the patient's stress hyperglycemia on admission
Dexamethasone may mask signs of infection; monitor for occult infection peri-operatively
Levetiracetam may cause behavioral side effects (irritability, mood changes); monitor in the perioperative setting
Long-term dexamethasone use requires taper to avoid adrenal insufficiency; plan steroid taper after definitive treatment
alternatives: Lacosamide as alternative antiepileptic if levetiracetam not tolerated (also non-enzyme-inducing)
Avoid enzyme-inducing antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) as they may interact with future chemotherapy (temozolomide)

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during concurrent chemoradiation
interactions: No significant interaction with levetiracetam (levetiracetam is non-enzyme-inducing)
Dexamethasone: no direct pharmacokinetic interaction, but steroids may reduce temozolomide efficacy in some preclinical models (clinical significance uncertain)
warnings: Myelosuppression is the dose-limiting toxicity; obtain CBC weekly during concurrent phase and before each adjuvant cycle
Pneumocystis pneumonia prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole required during concurrent phase — NOTE: patient has sulfonamide allergy, use pentamidine inhalation or dapsone as alternative
Hepatotoxicity: monitor liver function tests periodically
Teratogenic: contraception required (not applicable in this male patient's age group, but counsel if relevant)
output.formulary_status: On formulary; requires prior authorization for adjuvant phase dosing
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) as alternative alkylating agent if temozolomide not tolerated
Bevacizumab may be added at recurrence

differential.key_distinguishing: Typically more spherical with thinner ring enhancement at the gray-white junction; no known primary malignancy; CT staging negative for occult primary
Would show restricted diffusion centrally (pus), thin smooth ring enhancement, and clinical signs of infection; patient is afebrile with normal WBC and CRP
Typically homogeneous enhancement, periventricular location, immunocompromised host; necrosis uncommon in immunocompetent patients
Would show open ring enhancement, less mass effect for size, younger patient, possible MS history; incomplete ring enhancement pattern absent here
Typically non-enhancing, FLAIR bright without central necrosis, slower clinical course, younger patient; the ring enhancement and necrosis in this case exclude low-grade

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast to characterize the lesion identified on CT
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontal mass with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, surrounding vasogenic edema, and midline shift — highly suggestive of glioblastoma

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate seizure activity and epileptogenic potential given new-onset seizure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal slowing over the right frontal region with sharp waves indicating epileptogenic potential in peri-tumoral cortex

optimal_actions.action: Obtain baseline laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, coagulation, and hepatic function for surgical planning and chemotherapy baseline
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal labs providing adequate baseline for surgery and future chemotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone for peritumoral edema and levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis
Request MR perfusion to assess vascularity of the enhancing components
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in the enhancing rim consistent with high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Request CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out occult primary malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative staging CT, supporting primary brain tumor diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neurosurgery for maximal safe resection with tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling
Check drug interactions for planned medications to ensure no enzyme-inducing antiepileptics are used
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Levetiracetam is non-enzyme-inducing and safe to use with future temozolomide; avoid phenytoin/carbamazepine

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the mass lesion
Initiate dexamethasone for peritumoral edema and mass effect management
Initiate antiepileptic therapy (non-enzyme-inducing agent such as levetiracetam)
Urgent neurosurgical referral for tissue diagnosis and surgical planning
Obtain molecular profiling including IDH status and MGMT promoter methylation on tissue specimen
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture in the setting of a large mass with significant midline shift (risk of herniation)
Use of enzyme-inducing antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) which interact with temozolomide and other chemotherapy agents
Administration of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for PCP prophylaxis (patient has sulfonamide allergy)
Delay in initiating dexamethasone when there is significant mass effect and midline shift
Stereotactic radiosurgery as sole treatment for a lesion of this size without tissue diagnosis
key_reasoning_points: The progressive course over weeks with headache, focal motor symptoms, and new-onset seizure in a middle-aged adult is a classic presentation concerning for an intracranial mass lesion

The initial presentation of tingling, numbness, and involuntary hand movements was likely focal sensorimotor seizures originating from the right frontal/parietal cortex, not peripheral nerve compression
MRI features of thick irregular ring enhancement with central necrosis, surrounding vasogenic edema, and infiltrative margins are highly characteristic of glioblastoma
Restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim (indicating high cellularity) rather than centrally (which would suggest abscess) is a key distinguishing feature
Single dominant lesion with no systemic malignancy on staging workup supports a primary brain tumor rather than metastasis
Early papilledema and significant midline shift (9 mm) indicate elevated intracranial pressure, making dexamethasone initiation critical and lumbar puncture contraindicated
Non-enzyme-inducing antiepileptics should be selected to avoid interference with future chemotherapy regimens
Patient's sulfonamide allergy must be noted for PCP prophylaxis planning during concurrent temozolomide therapy, requiring alternative agents such as pentamidine or dapsone

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward presentation with classic findings: progressive headache, contralateral motor deficit, and new-onset seizure in a middle-aged adult with CT showing a large enhancing mass with midline shift. MRI findings are textbook for glioblastoma with thick irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, and surrounding edema. Minimal confounders.
